sect1.title: Re-sequenced Codes
sect1.para: 🔊 An audio speaker symbol means the service is included in Appendix T of the AMA CPT manual and may be reported with modifier 93 when provided via audio-only telecommunications technology (eg, telephone).

★ A star means the service is reported as a telemedicine service and is included in CPT Appendix P as a code to which modifier 95 (synchronous telemedicine service) may be appended to indicate that the service was rendered via real-time telemedicine services using audiovisual technology.
CPT appendixes provide quick references to modifiers; a summary of new, revised, and deleted codes; add-on and modifier-exempt codes; and other procedure code references.
CPT includes an index of procedures used to find codes and code ranges to help guide the user to the correct section(s) of codes.

listitem.para: Terms in the index cover procedures and services, organs and anatomical sites, conditions, synonyms, eponyms, and abbreviations (eg, EEG for electroencephalography).
Codes should not be selected from the index without verification of the code descriptor and reporting instructions in the appropriate section of the manual (eg, Evaluation and Management section).
Many diagnostic services require both technical and professional components.
It is important to understand that the technical component (eg, obtaining an electrocardiogram) leads to results or findings.
The interpretation and creation of a report of the results or findings are a professional service. Some services specifically require a professional's interpretation and report.
Other services include only a technical component and require only documentation of the results or findings (eg, scoring of a developmental screening instrument).

Codes and modifiers exist to report the technical or professional component when the complete service is not provided by the same individual or provider (eg, facility provides technical component and physician provides professional component).

para.emphasis: Never report a code for a global service without a modifier when only the technical or professional component is provided. Refer to Chapter 2 for discussion of appropriate modifiers (eg, 26, TC).

listitem.para: AOCs (marked with a + before the code) are always performed in addition to a primary service and are never reported as stand-alone services. AOCs describe additional intraservice work and are not valued to include preservice and postservice work like most other codes.

para.emphasis: Code 99459 is never reported without one of the E/M codes listed in the parenthetical instruction having been provided and reported on the same DOS.

sect1.para: #+99459 Pelvic examination (List separately in addition to the code of the outpatient E/M service) (Use 99459 in conjunction with 99202–99205, 99212–99215, 99242–99245, 99345, 99383–99387, or 99393–99397.)

Teaching Point: The inclusionary parenthetical notes following the AOCs are not all inclusive but, rather, include the typical base code(s) and not every possible reportable code combination. The parenthetical note in the descriptor of 99459, however, instructs that this code is reported in addition to outpatient E/M service codes (ie, not codes in other categories).

sect2.title: National Drug Code
sect2.para: NDCs are universal product identifiers for prescription drugs, including immunizations and insulin products. Codes are 10-digit, 3-segment numbers that identify the product, labeler, and trade package size. Medicare, Medicaid, and other government payers (eg, Tricare), as well as some private payers, may require the use of NDCs when reporting medication and immunization product codes.

listitem.para: The HIPAA standards for reporting NDCs do not align with the 10-digit format; they require an 11-digit code.
Conversion to an 11-digit code in 5-digit-4-digit-2-digit format is required.
Leading zeros are added to the appropriate segment to accomplish the 5-4-2 format, asillustrated in Table 1-3.

row.entry: Gardasil 9 (human papillomavirus vaccine) carton often 0.5-mL single-dose prefilled syringes
Ceftriaxone 250 mg per vial powder single-dose

sect2.para: It is important to verify each payer's requirements for NDC reporting. A payer may require either of the following:

listitem.para: The NDC that is provided on the outer packaging when immunization products or another drug is supplied inbulk packages
The NDC from the vial or prefilled syringe that was administered
A qualifier (N4) precedes the NDC number on the claim form (eg, N400006412102).
The correct number of NDC units must also be included on claims.
The NDC units are often different from HCPCS or CPT units.
For most payers, NDC units are reported as grams (GR), milligrams (ME), milliliters (ML), or units (UN).
The qualifiers GR, ME, ML, and UNare reported before the number of NDC units on a claim to indicate the measure.

sect2.para: For examples of how NDC codes and units are reported on a claim, refer to Figure 1-1.

If you are not currently reporting medications with NDCs, be sure to coordinate the requirements with your billing company or billing software company. For more information and updates on NDCs, visit www.fda.gov/drugs/drug-approvals-and-databases/national-drug-code-directory.

listitem.para: Diagnoses should be assigned by the physician or QHP indicating a principal (primary) diagnosis that best explains the reason with the highest risk of morbidity or mortality for the patient encounter, followed by other conditions that were managed or that affected management at this encounter. Code assignment may take place in conjunction with documentation or later, depending on the workflow processes of the practice.
Assignment of the specific diagnosis code by clinical or administrative staff should be done under the physician's or reporting provider's supervision.
